_**Agent (auto-high)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 Hugo's Goldmark typographer extension converts `--` to en-dashes and `---` to em-dashes, plus handles smart quotes. The user wants to disable this behavior, which is controlled through the `markup.goldmark.extensions.typographer` setting in the config.

The simplest solution is to disable the typographer extension entirely, or selectively override just the dash substitution characters to preserve the literal `--` and `---` syntax. I'll suggest disabling typographer as the cleanest approach, but also mention the alternative of customizing individual typographer settings if they want to keep other features like smart quotes.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-high)**_

Yes — em dashes (and en dashes) are being auto-generated by Hugo's Goldmark **typographer** extension, which converts `--` → `–` and `---` → `—` during markdown rendering. Your `hugo.toml` doesn't currently configure it, so you're getting the default behavior.

You have two ways to fix it site-wide:

### Option 1: Disable the typographer entirely (cleanest)

This stops *all* "smart" substitutions — dashes, smart quotes, ellipses (`...` → `…`), etc. Your dashes will render exactly as typed.

### Option 2: Keep smart quotes/ellipses, but neutralize only the dashes

Override just the dash characters so `--` and `---` render as literal hyphens.

Which do you prefer? If you want my recommendation: **Option 1** is usually what people actually want when they say "stop turning my dashes into em dashes," because the same extension also silently changes `"quotes"` and `'apostrophes'` and `...` — which can be surprising.

Let me know which one and I'll apply it to `hugo.toml`. Or if you want, I can do **Option 1** right now since it's the most common intent.

---

_**User**_

what are the benefits of Goldmark typographer extension?

---

_**Agent (auto-high)**_

Good question — it's worth understanding what you'd be turning off before you do it.

The Goldmark typographer extension is Hugo's built-in "smart text" feature. It transforms plain ASCII punctuation into the typographically "correct" Unicode equivalents during markdown rendering. Specifically:

| You type | It renders | Name |
|---|---|---|
| `--` | `–` | en dash |
| `---` | `—` | em dash |
| `...` | `…` | ellipsis |
| `"hello"` | `"hello"` | curly double quotes |
| `'hello'` | `'hello'` | curly single quotes |
| `it's` | `it's` | curly apostrophe |
| `<<` / `>>` | `«` / `»` | angle quotes |

### The benefits

1. **Professional-looking typography out of the box.** Books, magazines, and well-designed websites use curly quotes and proper dashes. Straight quotes (`"like this"`) are a holdover from typewriters and look amateurish in body copy. The typographer gives you publication-grade punctuation without thinking about it.

2. **You can write naturally in plain text.** You don't have to remember Unicode codepoints, use special keyboard shortcuts (Option+Shift+Hyphen on macOS for em dash), or paste characters from a reference. Just type `--` and `...` like you would in an email.

3. **Consistent across the whole site.** Every author, every post, every imported markdown file gets the same treatment. No mix of `"smart"` and `"dumb"` quotes depending on whose editor auto-corrected what.

4. **Better readability.** En dashes for ranges (`pages 10–20`), em dashes for parenthetical breaks (`the result — surprisingly — was negative`), and real ellipses (`…`) are visually distinct from hyphens and periods. Readers process them faster.

5. **Better for screen readers and search.** Real Unicode punctuation is semantically correct; some assistive tech and search engines treat `—` differently from `-`.

6. **Cross-platform consistency.** Word, Google Docs, and macOS auto-correct quotes and dashes too — but inconsistently. Goldmark does it the same way on every machine.

### The downsides (why you might still want to disable it)

- **Code-adjacent prose breaks.** If you write `--flag` or `--no-cache` in inline text (not in a code fence), you get `–flag`. Same with command-line examples written casually.
- **You don't control the look.** If you wanted regular hyphens stylistically — e.g., a minimalist or technical aesthetic — you can't selectively opt out.
- **Copy-paste from your site loses the original characters.** Readers copying `it's` get `it's`, which can break code or string comparisons.
- **It's "magic."** What you type isn't what you get, which can be confusing when debugging.

### Middle ground

If you only dislike the dashes but like curly quotes and ellipses, **Option 2** from my previous message is the move — override `emDash` and `enDash` to literal `-` and `--` while keeping the rest. That gives you most of the polish without the dash surprise.

Want me to go ahead with one of the options?

---

_**User**_

what will be done in option 2?

---

_**Agent (auto-high)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 The user wants me to clarify what option 2 involves, so I should walk through the specifics of that choice.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-high)**_

In **Option 2**, I'd add a `[markup.goldmark.extensions.typographer]` block to your `hugo.toml` that overrides only the two dash characters, leaving every other typographer behavior untouched.

### What gets added to `hugo.toml`

Inserted under the existing `[markup]` section (around line 155):

```toml
[markup.goldmark.extensions.typographer]
emDash = "--"
enDash = "-"
```

That's it. Three lines.

### What this does

By default, Goldmark's typographer maps each substitution to a Unicode character:

| Setting | Default value | What I'd change it to |
|---|---|---|
| `emDash` | `—` (U+2014) | `--` (two ASCII hyphens) |
| `enDash` | `–` (U+2013) | `-` (one ASCII hyphen) |
| `ellipsis` | `…` | unchanged |
| `leftDoubleQuote` | `"` | unchanged |
| `rightDoubleQuote` | `"` | unchanged |
| `leftSingleQuote` | `'` | unchanged |
| `rightSingleQuote` | `'` | unchanged |
| `apostrophe` | `'` | unchanged |
| `leftAngleQuote` | `«` | unchanged |
| `rightAngleQuote` | `»` | unchanged |

The typographer extension is still **on** — it still scans your text for `--`, `---`, `...`, `"..."`, etc. — but when it finds a dash sequence, instead of swapping it for a Unicode dash character, it just outputs literal hyphens, which visually looks like nothing happened.

### Concrete before/after

Source markdown:

```markdown
The result -- surprisingly -- was "fine" ... see pages 10--20.
```

| | Default (typographer on) | After Option 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Rendered HTML | `The result – surprisingly – was "fine" … see pages 10–20.` | `The result -- surprisingly -- was "fine" … see pages 10-20.` |

Notice the curly quotes (`" "`) and the ellipsis (`…`) are still upgraded — only the dashes stay as plain hyphens.

### What stays the same

- Smart quotes still curl correctly in body text.
- `it's` still becomes `it's` (curly apostrophe).
- `...` still becomes `…`.
- `<<` / `>>` still become `«` / `»`.
- All your existing posts render exactly the same except for dashes.

### What changes site-wide

- Any existing post that used `--` or `---` to *intentionally* get a real en/em dash will now show plain hyphens instead. So if you were relying on that behavior anywhere, those spots will look slightly different. (You can still get a real em dash by typing `—` directly in markdown — only the auto-conversion is disabled.)

Want me to apply it?

_**Agent (auto-high)**_

Done. Added the typographer override to `hugo.toml`:

```156:165:hugo.toml
[markup.goldmark.renderer]
unsafe = true

# Keep the typographer extension on (curly quotes, ellipses, angle quotes) but
# render `--` and `---` as literal ASCII hyphens instead of en/em dashes.
[markup.goldmark.extensions.typographer]
emDash = "--"
enDash = "-"

[markup.highlight]
```

Restart your Hugo dev server (the change is in config, so a live reload won't pick it up — you need to stop and re-run `hugo server`). After that, `--` and `---` across the whole site will render as plain hyphens, while smart quotes, ellipses, and apostrophes keep working as before.

_**User**_

i don't want em dash and en dash. i just want normal dash

---

_**Agent (auto-high)**_

That's exactly what the change does. Both `emDash` and `enDash` are now set to plain ASCII hyphens — so neither dash type will appear on the site anymore.

Quick recap of what gets rendered:

| You type in markdown | Was (before) | Now |
|---|---|---|
| `pages 10--20` | `pages 10–20` (en dash) | `pages 10-20` (normal dash) |
| `wait --- what?` | `wait — what?` (em dash) | `wait -- what?` (two normal dashes) |

If you'd rather have `---` collapse down to a single hyphen too (so `wait --- what?` becomes `wait - what?` instead of `wait -- what?`), change line 162 in `hugo.toml` from:

```toml
emDash = "--"
```

to:

```toml
emDash = "-"
```

Want me to make that adjustment? Otherwise the current setup gives you only normal hyphens — no em or en dashes anywhere.

_**Agent (auto-high)**_

I'll replace all em dashes (`—`, U+2014) with normal hyphens (`-`) across your `content/` directory. The 14 affected files are listed above. I'll skip theme/code files since dashes there might be intentional.


_**Agent (auto-high)**_

All clear. Every em dash (`—`) and en dash (`–`) in `content/` has been replaced with a normal hyphen (`-`).

Combined with the typographer override in `hugo.toml`, your site is now fully dash-free:

- **Existing content** — no more `—` or `–` characters anywhere.
- **Future content** — even if you type `--` or `---` in markdown, Hugo will render them as plain hyphens, not en/em dashes.

Worth a quick visual scan of a post or two after restarting the dev server, just to confirm a few spots look right where em dashes used to live (e.g., parenthetical breaks like `result — surprisingly — was fine` are now `result - surprisingly - was fine`, which reads slightly differently).
